coredata.dc:title: The reversible surface redox of polymer dots for the assay of total antioxidant capacity in food samples
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Wei
Xiang, Yun
Xu, Jiabin
He, Xiaofeng
Zhao, Peng
coredata.dc:description: In this paper, polymer dots (Pdots) were synthesized by one-step nanoprecipitation method from cationic conjugated polymer and hyaluronic acid, it was found that the fluorescence of Pdots could be reversibly quenched by NaClO. Mechanism study indicated that the reversible quenching was resulted from the reversible surface redox process of Pdots with NaClO and antioxidants. Based on this phenomenon, a reusable determination strategy of total antioxidant capacity (TAC) was developed. Three nonenzymatic antioxidants and two enzymatic antioxidants were selected as model targets to test its detection ability, and the detection linear ranges are 0.1–50 μM for glutathione and ascorbic acid, 0.5–10 μM for N-acetyl-l-cysteine, 1–30 U mL−1 for superoxide dismutase and 1–20 U mL−1 for catalase. Compared with standard assay method of total antioxidant capacity, this Pdots-NaClO system simplified operation process and meanwhile increased sensitivity. Moreover, this strategy has a great potential in the assay of food quality, as it was successfully applied to investigate TAC in fruit and tea samples with satisfactory recoveries ranging from 88.4% to 114.2%, and the relative standard deviation of the obtained TAC value was from 3.2% to 9.9%.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Polymer dots
Fluorescence sensing
Reversible surface redox
Antioxidant
Food samples

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Determination of antioxidants with the CCP-HA Pdots-hypochlorite system
The analysis of total antioxidant capacity in food samples
Determination of antioxidants based on the reversible surface redox of CCP-HA Pdots
Determination of other antioxidants using the CCP-HA Pdots
Determination of total antioxidant capacity of food samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) The TEM image of CCP-HA Pdots and the inset shows the corresponding size distributions. (B) The fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) spectrum of CCP (red line) and CCP-HA Pdots (blue line). (C) The fluorescence excitation (red line) and emission spectra of CCP-HA Pdots without (blue line) or with (purple line) 200 μM NaClO. (D) Fluorescence spectra of CCP-HA Pdots with different concentrations (0, 5, 10, 20, 50, 100, 150, 200 and 300 μM) of NaClO under excitation wavelength of 370 nm in 10 mM PBS (pH 7.2) and the inset shows plots of the fluorescence intensity at 424 nm versus NaClO concentration .(For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(A) The fluorescence emission spectra of CCP-HA Pdots with 200 μM NaClO, 50 μM GSH and 200 μM NaClO with 50 μM GSH. Inset: the photograph of CCP-HA Pdots in the absence (a) and presence of NaClO (b) and in the presence of NaClO and GSH (c) under 365 nm UV light. (B) The fluorescence spectra of Pdots-ClO− system in the presence of 200 μM NaClO solution with different concentrations of GSH (0, 0.1, 0.2, 0.5, 1.0, 2.0, 5.0, 10.0, 20.0, 30.0, 40.0 and 50.0 μM) in 10 mM PBS (pH 7.2). (C) The assay of GSH by Pdots-ClO− system in the second determination cycle. (D) Plots of the fluorescence intensity at 424 nm of Pdots-ClO− system with different concentration of GSH in the first (black spots) and second determination cycles (red spots). Inset: the corresponding linear fitting curve of the GSH concentrations from 0 to 2 μM .(For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(A) Fluorescence spectra of Pdots-ClO− system in the presence of 200 μΜ NaClO and different concentrations of ascorbic acid (0, 0.1, 0.2, 0.5, 1.0, 2.0, 5.0, 10.0, 20.0 and 50.0 μΜ) in 10 mM PBS (pH 7.2). (B) The corresponding linear fitting curve of fluorescence intensity at 424 nm with AA concentration. (C) Fluorescence spectra of Pdots-ClO− system in the presence of 200 μΜ NaClO and different concentrations of NAC (0, 0.5, 1.0, 2.0, 5.0, 10.0, 20.0 and 50.0 μΜ) in 10 mM PBS (pH 7.2). (D) The corresponding linear fitting curve of fluorescence intensity at 424 nm with NAC concentration.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of Pdots-ClO− system in the presence of 100 μΜ NaClO and different concentrations of SOD (0, 1.0, 2.0, 5.0, 10.0, 20.0, 30.0, 40.0, 50.0 and 60.0 U mL−1) in 10 mM PBS (pH = 7.2). (B) Linear fitting curve of fluorescence intensity at 424 nm with SOD concentration. (C) Fluorescence spectra of Pdots-ClO− system in the presence of 100 μΜ NaClO and different concentrations of CAT (0, 0.25, 0.50, 0.75, 1.0 5.0, 10.0, 20.0 and 50.0 U mL−1) in 10 mM PBS (pH 7.2). (D) Linear fitting curve of fluorescence intensity at 424 nm with CAT concentration.

(A) The normalized increased intensity (ΔF) of Pdots-ClO− system with the addition 1.25 mM metal ions (Ba2+, Ca2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Mg2+, Co2+, Na+, K+, Ni2+), 0.25 mM formic acid (HCOOH), oxalic acid (H2C2O4) and glucose, 2 μM nonenzymatic antioxidants (GSH, AA, NAC) and 20 U mL−1 enzymatic (SOD, CAT). (B) The relative fluorescence intensity of Pdots-ClO− system in the presence of 200 μΜ NaClO and the addition 100 μL extracted solution of fruit (kiwifruit, lemon, pomelo) and teas (green tea and black tea) with changed times. The relative fluorescence intensity of Pdots-ClO− system in the presence of 200 μΜ NaClO and the addition 100 μL extracted solution of (C) lemon and (D) green tea storing in different conditions.
Illustration of determination strategy of total antioxidant capacity based on the reversible surface redox of conjugated polymer dots.
Comparison of the present work with other reported methods for the detection of glutathione.
Linear regression equations, square of correlation coefficient (R 2), and linear concentration ranges of the tested compounds with respect to CCP-HA Pdots-hypochlorite (n = 3).

row.entry: Linear regression equation and correlation coefficient (R 2)

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The determination results of GSH under the second cycle using the CCP-HA Pdots-hypochlorite system.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The determination of total antioxidant capacity (TAC) and recovery values from spiked different concentrations of AA in real samples. (n = 3).

head.ce:title: The reversible surface redox of polymer dots for the assay of total antioxidant capacity in food samples

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Division of Vascular and Interventional Radiology, Department of General Surgery, Nanfang Hospital, Southern Medical University, Guangzhou, Guangdong 510515, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Division of Vascular and Interventional Radiology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Division of Vascular and Interventional Radiology, Department of General Surgery, Nanfang Hospital, Southern Medical University, Guangzhou, Guangdong 510515, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of New Drug Screening, School of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Southern Medical University, Guangzhou, Guangdong 510515, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of New Drug Screening

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bGuangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of New Drug Screening, School of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Southern Medical University, Guangzhou, Guangdong 510515, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this paper, polymer dots (Pdots) were synthesized by one-step nanoprecipitation method from cationic conjugated polymer and hyaluronic acid, it was found that the fluorescence of Pdots could be reversibly quenched by NaClO. Mechanism study indicated that the reversible quenching was resulted from the reversible surface redox process of Pdots with NaClO and antioxidants. Based on this phenomenon, a reusable determination strategy of total antioxidant capacity (TAC) was developed. Three nonenzymatic antioxidants and two enzymatic antioxidants were selected as model targets to test its detection ability, and the detection linear ranges are 0.1–50 μM for glutathione and ascorbic acid, 0.5–10 μM for N-acetyl-l-cysteine, 1–30 U mL−1 for superoxide dismutase and 1–20 U mL−1 for catalase. Compared with standard assay method of total antioxidant capacity, this Pdots-NaClO system simplified operation process and meanwhile increased sensitivity. Moreover, this strategy has a great potential in the assay of food quality, as it was successfully applied to investigate TAC in fruit and tea samples with satisfactory recoveries ranging from 88.4% to 114.2%, and the relative standard deviation of the obtained TAC value was from 3.2% to 9.9%.

ce:section.ce:para: Reactive oxygen species (ROS) generated from oxidation reactions in the human body may cause damage to DNA, protein and lipids by starting chain reactions, resulting in many pathogenic process [1–3]. Antioxidants are substances with reducibility that can terminate these chain reactions by scavenging reactive oxygen species [4 , 5]. They have attracted great attentions in the prevention of oxidative stress related diseases and application in nutrition and medicine. Total antioxidant capacity (TAC) is a resultant measure of the ability of all antioxidants present in samples, it has served as one of standards in the quality assay of foods samples [6 , 7]. Appropriate evaluation of TAC could guide us to select a reasonable diet for modulating oxidative stress [8]. What's more, the antioxidant values of foods, biological samples, and other commercial products can be meaningfully compared, and variations between products can be controlled by suitable analysis methods of assay of TAC [9]. Therefore, development of new and effective techniques with high selectivity and accuracy for the measurement of antioxidant capacity of foods or biological samples is desired.

In order to sensitively detect TAC of real samples, a variety of methods have been constructed including chromatography [10], spectrophotometry [11,12], electrochemistry [13] and chemiluminescence [14]. Among them, fluorescent methods based on novel nanomaterials such quantum dots [15–19], carbon dots [20–23] and gold nanoclusters [24 , 25] have attracted much more attentions for their multiple advantages. In recent years, conjugated polymer dots (Pdots) exhibited extraordinary properties including high brightness, excellent photostability, fast emission rates, good biocompatibility and minimal toxicity [26–29]. Pdots with excellent properties have been successfully used for the applications in biosensing [30 , 31], fluorescence imaging [32 , 33], cell labeling [34], drug delivery and cancer therapy [35 , 36]. For example, Wang et al. reported a one-step method to synthesize Pdots via controlling hydrothermal treatment of PEG, and successfully determined to temperature and Fe3+ in real water sample [37]. Pdots can be dispersed evenly in the aqueous solution without the modification of ligand and has a good stability because of a plenty of hydrophilic groups on its surface [38]. The excellent characteristic endows Pdots with great potential to be used to construct a novel fluorescence detection method of TAC.

Herein, cationic conjugated polymer (CCP) and hyaluronic acid (HA) have been used to construct conjugated polymer dots (CCP-HA Pdots) through one-step nanoprecipitation method. The determination strategy is shown in Scheme 1. Interestingly, it has been found that the fluorescence of CCP-HA Pdots could be quenched after the addition of hypochlorite while recovered gradually in the presence of antioxidants. Mechanism study revealed that this phenomenon was resulted from the reversible surface redox process of CCP-HA Pdots. Next, this reversible surface redox process was utilized to assay nonenzymatic and enzymatic antioxidants with satisfying results. Furthermore, TAC of different real samples (i.e. AA as a representative antioxidant) have been measured and recovery tests were further carried on. The detection results by this work were compared with standard ferric ion reducing antioxidant power (FRAP) method. Experimental results indicated that the determination strategy of TAC not only simplified operation process (a complete measurement within 10 min), increased sensitivity (a detect limit of 75 nM of AA), but also had comparable assay results with standard FRAP method.

Cationic conjugated polymer (CCP), poly (9, 9-bis (6′-N,N,N-trimethylammonium) hexyl) fluorenylene phenylene (PF) was synthesized according to our previously reported method [39]. Hyaluronic acid (HA) and glutathione (GSH) were purchased from Sangon Biotech Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Superoxide dismutase (SOD) from bovine erythrocytes and catalase (CAT) from bovine liver were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (USA). Ascorbic acid (AA), N-acetyl-l-cysteine (NAC), sodium hypochlorite (NaClO) and other reagents were purchased from Aladdin Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All reagents were of analytical grade and used as received without any further purification. Real samples including fruits and teas were obtained from local supermarket (Guangzhou, China). The water used throughout all experiments were purified through a Millipore system (18.2 MΩ cm).

The fluorescence spectra and UV–vis absorption of CCP-HA Pdots were recorded with an RF-5301PC fluorescence spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan) and an UV-5500 spectrometer of Shanghai Metash instruments Co. Ltd, respectively. The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of CCP-HA Pdots were observed using a JEM-2100F high-resolution transmission electron microscope (JEOL, Japan). Zeta potential and dynamic light scattering (DLS) were performed on a laser granulometer (Malvern Zetasizer Nano ZS90). The FT-IR spectra were recorded by a Tensor 27 infrared spectrometer (Bruke, Germany). The fluorescence lifetime and the fluorescence quantum yield of CCP-HA Pdots can be recorded by steady/transient fluorescence spectrometer FLS 980 (Edinburgh, British).

Firstly, 325 mg of 2, 7-dibromo-9, 9-bis (6′-bromohexyl) fluorine (0.5 mmol), 82.9 mg of 1, 4-phenyldiboronic acid (0.5 mmol), 830 mg of potassium carbonate (6 mmol) and 7 mg of 1,1′-bis (diphenylphosphino) ferrocene-palladium (II) dichloride dichloromethane complex (Pd(dppf) Cl2) were added in a 50 mL round bottom flask which contains 6 mL tetrahydrofuran (THF) and 3 mL of deionized water. Secondly, the reaction vessel was degassed and filled with argon. Finally, the mixture was stirred at 85 °C for 24 h, and cooled down to room temperature, and then precipitated into methanol to get the neutral compound B. The compound B was filtered and washed with methanol and acetone, and then dried under vacuum for 24 h.

After that, 60 mg of the neutral compound B was placed in a 25 mL round bottle flask with 10 mL of tetrahydrofuran (THF), and then condensed trimethylamine (2 mL) was added dropwise to the solution at −78 °C. The mixture was reacted at room temperature for 24 h. The precipitate was re-dissolved in water (10 mL). After the mixture was cooled down to −78 °C, the mixture was stirred continuously for 24 h at room temperature upon the addition of about 2 mL extra trimethylamine. Compound PF, namely cationic conjugated polymer (CCP), was precipitated with acetone after removing most of the solvent for further use.

CCP-HA Pdots were synthesized by nanoprecipitation method [40]. Typically, cationic conjugated polymer (CCP) and hyaluronic acid (HA) were first dissolved in THF to obtain 1 mg mL−1 stock solution, respectively. Then, 250 μL of CCP (1 mg mL−1), 50 μL of HA (1 mg mL−1) were mixed together in total volume 5 mL of THF. The mixture was sonicated for 10 min, then adding into rapidly 10 mL of ultrapure water and sonicated continually for 10 min. After that, THF and part of the water were removed by rotary evaporation at 55°C about 20 min. CCP-HA Pdots solution was filtered through a 0.22 μm filter to remove any larger aggregates. The resulting Pdots solution was stored in a refrigerator at 4°C for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of antioxidants with the CCP-HA Pdots-hypochlorite system
ce:section.ce:para: Nonenzymatic antioxidants were detected as follows: 300 μL CCP-HA Pdots solution (diluted 25-folds by phosphate buffer saline (PBS, 10 mM, pH 7.2)) was gently mixed with 200 μM NaClO aqueous solution, and then 100 μL different concentrations of representative antioxidants (GSH, AA and NAC) in PBS was added and mixed. For the determination of enzymatic antioxidants, 300 μL CCP-HA Pdots solution (diluted 60-folds by 10 mM PBS, pH 7.2) was gently mixed with 100 μM NaClO solution, and then 100 μL different concentrations of representative antioxidants (SOD and CAT) in PBS was added and mixed. After incubation at room temperature for 5 min, the fluorescence spectra of the mixture at 424 nm (excitation at 370 nm) was detected, the slit width of excitation and emission were set at 5 nm and 5 nm, respectively.

In order to research the selectivity of the CCP-HA Pdots-hypochlorite system for metal ions and other interfering substances, the fluorescence intensity changes of the CCP-HA Pdots under the presence of metal ions (Ba2+, Ca2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Mg2+, Co2+, Na+, K+, Ni2+), small molecules (glucose, formic acid (HCOOH), oxalic acid (H2C2O4)) were detected. The experimental procedures were as follows: 300 μL CCP-HA Pdots solution (diluted 60-folds by 10 mM PBS, pH 7.2) was gently mixed with 100 μM NaClO solution, and then 100 μL of interferents solution in PBS buffer (10 mM, pH 7.2) was added, the final concentrations of interferents were 1.25 mM metal ions, 0.25 mM formic acid, oxalic acid and glucose. Then, the fluorescence spectra of the mixture were recorded following the same procedure for detection of antioxidants mentioned above.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The analysis of total antioxidant capacity in food samples
ce:section.ce:para: The stability of CCP-HA Pdots in real samples was investigated before the determination of TAC. The method is as follows: the Pdots were diluted 25-folds with different samples solution by fresh fruit juices or teas, and their fluorescence intensity monitoring after incubating for 30 min. For the feasibility of the CCP-HA Pdots-hypochlorite system, the edible portions of about 0.2 g in fruit samples were put into the grinder with 25 mL phosphate buffer saline (10 mM, pH = 7.2) as extracts, and were quickly mashed into juice and then insoluble portions of the fruits were discarded. The extracted solution was centrifuged at 4000 rpm min−1 for 10 min to remove tiny solid suspensions and other impurities and transferred into volumetric flask and diluted with PBS to 50 mL. The supernatant was stored at 4°C and was protected from light by covering them with aluminum foil until analysis. For the determination of TAC in teas, about 0.2 g of green/black tea was soaked in 50 mL of PBS for 12 h. The supernatant after centrifugation of extracts, was placed in 4°C and was protected from light for further use.

In this work, CCP-HA Pdots was synthesized by an ultrasonication-assisted coprecipitation method. The prepared CCP-HA Pdots displayed uniform nanosphere morphologies from TEM image (Fig. 1 A). The mean diameter of CCP-HA Pdots was 6.2 ± 0.4 nm (calculated from 145 nanoparticles) shown in inset of Fig. 1A. These results were also consistent with the dynamic light scattering (DLS) measurements (Fig. S1). In addition, the zeta potentials showed that the surface charge of CCP-HA Pdots (5.83 mV) decreased compared to that of CCP (30.96 mV), suggesting that HA molecules were successfully assembled onto CCP via electrostatic interaction (Fig. S2). In Fig. 1B, the fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) spectra showed that CCP exhibited the characteristic peaks at 2924 cm−1, 2850 cm−1 which are assigned to v (-CH2) stretching vibration of methene groups. The peaks at 1597 cm−1, 1480 cm−1 and 1423 cm−1 correspond to v (CC) the skeleton vibration of the benzene ring. The peak at 1120 cm−1 corresponds to δ (CH) stretching vibration while the peaks at 826 cm−1 are ascribed to γ (=CH) bending vibration in benzene. These results indicated that CCP was successfully synthesized. The FT-IR spectrum of CCP-HA Pdots is slightly difference compared with CCP. A broad and strong peak at 3427 cm−1 is ascribed to v (OH) stretching vibration of the adsorbed water, carboxyl groups, and hydroxyl groups from HA. The characteristic peaks at 1631 cm−1 and 1130 cm−1 are assigned to v (CO) vibration of carboxyl groups and v (CO) stretching vibration in COH groups from HA. These results also confirmed the successful construction of CCP-HA Pdots.

To examine the fluorescence properties of CCP-HA Pdots, excitation and emission spectra were first conducted. As we can see in Fig. 1C, the maximum excitation wavelength was located at 370 nm, which was used as the excitation wavelength in the following experiments. Upon excitation at 370 nm, there were two emission peaks in the fluorescence spectrum of CCP-HA Pdots, a strong peak at 424 nm and a weak peak at 450 nm. The UV–vis spectrum shows that CCP-HA Pdots have an obvious peak at 381 nm (Fig. S3). The fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of CCP-HA Pdots has been measured by integrating sphere. It could be calculated that the absolute QYs of CCP-HA Pdots is 25.28%. Interestingly, after 200 µM NaClO was added, the fluorescence of CCP-HA Pdots decreased about 76.7%. Furthermore, the reaction between CCP-HA Pdots and ClO− were found to be very quick and fluorescence intensity could reach equilibrium in 5 min after NaClO added (Fig. S4 A, B). In addition, the fluorescence intensity decreased gradually with the concentration of NaClO ranging from 0 to 300 µM after 5 min (Fig. 1D).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of antioxidants based on the reversible surface redox of CCP-HA Pdots
ce:section.ce:para: First, we have investigated the quenching mechanism of CCP-HA Pdots by ClO−. As there are plenty of hydroxyl (-OH) in hyaluronic acid, hydroxyl is also abundant on the surface of the Pdots. Many studies have proved that the fluorescence properties of Pdots were obviously affected by their surface states [41,42]. As we can see from Fig. S2, after reacted with ClO−, the decrease of zeta potential of CCP-HA Pdots from 5.83 mV to −1.30 mV implied that the surface states of CCP-HA Pdots have been obviously changed after the addition of 200 µM NaClO. Under the DLS examination, the average particle size of CCP-HA Pdots has no significant chang after treating with NaClO (Fig. S1), suggesting no obvious aggregation in the presence of NaClO. It also showed that the fluorescence quenching was caused by the oxidation of hydroxyl groups on the surface of CCP-HA Pdots rather than aggregation. Meanwhile, the similar fluorescence life time of CCP-HA Pdots with the addition of ClO−also indicated a steady quenching mechanism (Fig. S5) [43].

As shown in Fig. S6, the FT-IR spectrum of CCP-HA Pdots with 200 µM NaClO has a signification difference compared with CCP-HA Pdots. The characteristic peaks at 1631 cm−1 v(CO) was obviously strengthen after the oxidation of hydroxyl groups by hypochlorite. Therefore, we deduced that the quenching of CCP-HA Pdots by ClO− was resulted from the oxidation of the surface hydroxyl groups with ClO−.

Then, we have examined the effects of antioxidants to the oxidation induced fluorescence quenching of CCP-HA Pdots-ClO− system. Glutathione (GSH), which is capable of preventing damage to important cellular components caused by reactive oxygen species, was selected as a model target [44]. As shown in Fig. 2 A, after the addition of 50 µM GSH, the quenched fluorescence of CCP-HA Pdots by 200 µM NaClO could recover 111.0%. Under UV excitation at 365 nm, it could be seen that the aqueous solution turned from blue to nearly colorless to bright blue transparent (inset of Fig. 2A). Along with the fluorescent results, the zeta potential of CCP-HA Pdots with 50 µM GSH and 200 µM NaClO increased to 4.99 mV (Fig. S2) which was much closer to that on the original Pdots (5.83 mV). What's more, the FT-IR spectrum of CCP-HA Pdots with NaClO and GSH has changed (Fig. S6). The peaks at 3450 cm−1 v(–OH) was obviously strengthen while the peaks at 1631 cm−1 v(C=O) was obviously weakened, indicating that the oxidized hydroxyl group was reduced after the addition of GSH with reducibility. Based on the above results, it can be concluded that this “quenched-recovered” fluorescence of Pdots was resulted from the reversible surface redox process of CCP-HA Pdots.

Next, we have developed a reusable sensor of antioxidants with high sensitivity utilizing the reversible surface redox process of CCP-HA Pdots. In Fig. 2B, the fluorescence intensity of CCP-HA Pdots increased upon the addition of increased concentrations of GSH. Two linear range for monitoring GSH were from 0.1 µM to 1.0 µM and from 2 μM to 50 μM with a detection limit of 75 nM (3S/N) (Fig. 2D). Interestingly, it was found that after a determination of GSH utilizing Pdots-NaClO system, the CCP-HA Pdots could be used to the repeated detection of GSH with the addition of NaClO. As was shown in Fig. 2C, in the solution containing CCP-HA Pdots with 200 μM NaClO, 50 μM GSH, the addition of 200 μM NaClO could maintain the repeated determination of GSH, the linear range was from 0.1 µM to 2.0 µM with a detection limit 63 nM (3S/N) (Fig. 2D). Then, the potential of Pdots for the reversible detection of GSH were further examined, and in each detection cycle, the CCP-HA Pdots were separated by ultrafilters to ensure its sensitivity. In Fig. S7, after the first two detection cycles, there is still a good linearity between the fluorescent intensity and the concentration of GSH from 0.1 μM to 2 μM in the next three cycles. The above results indicated that the CCP-HA Pdots could be used for at least five repeated determinations of GSH, which is also an important advantage for this assay method [45,46]. The detection results of the proposed methods for glutathione have been compared with other analytical methods and listed in Table 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of other antioxidants using the CCP-HA Pdots
ce:section.ce:para: In order to examine the feasibility of this approach for detecting antioxidants, AA as a represent antioxidant was studied and then analyzed by CCP-HA Pdots and total antioxidant capacity assay kit with FRAP method, which served as a reference method. The concentrations of AA were controlled in 0.2, 0.4, 0.6, 0.8 and 1.0 mM. A Bland–Altman plot was constructed according to the measured results of two methods by software “MedCalc” and shown in Fig. S8. It can be seen that 96.7% of the points were within the 95% limits of agreement, indicating the proposed method and FRAP method have high consistency in determination of antioxidants. These results demonstrate that both nonenzymatic and enzymatic antioxidants can be detected by the CCP-HA Pdots-ClO− system.

Selectivity is an important index to evaluate the proposed method. Control experiments with the addition of metal ions and small molecules, such as Ba2+, Ca2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Mg2+, Co2+, Na+, K+, Ni2+, glucose, formic acid (HCOOH) and oxalic acid (H2C2O4) were carried out to evaluate the specificity of CCP-HA Pdots for detection of antioxidants. All the fluorescence spectra were obtained under excitation at 370 nm. As shown in Fig. 5
A, it was seen that the fluorescence intensity at 424 nm of CCP-HA Pdots-ClO− system was quickly increased upon antioxidants such as GSH, AA, NAC and SOD. While there was not obvious change by adding metal ions or other species into the system. It is worth noting that the addition of formic acid and oxalic acid can cause slightly enhancement due to their weaker reduction properties. However, the enhancement does not affect the whole determination process in comparison to antioxidants. These results demonstrated the high selectivity and specificity of the CCP-HA Pdots-ClO− system for determination of antioxidants over other substances.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of total antioxidant capacity of food samples
ce:section.ce:para: TAC of foods plays an important role in comparing antioxidant content of different foods and evaluating the inner qualities. Therefore, the feasibility of CCP-HA Pdots for qualitative and quantitative determination of antioxidants in food samples were investigated. The stability of the detection system has been examined by monitoring fluorescence intensity of CCP-HA Pdots after incubation for 30 min in the phosphate buffer saline, fruits juices (lemon, pomelo and kiwi fruit) and teas (green tea and black tea) as shown in Fig. S9. These results indicated that there was no obvious change in fluorescence intensity for the rest of samples except for a slight decrease in green tea, which suggested the Pdots-ClO− system has a relatively good stability in corresponding media. In addition, the TAC of real samples including fruit juices and teas were tested, the content of AA was used to represent TAC of samples. According to the standard curve of AA detection (Fig. 3B), the TAC values of these samples were estimated as shown in Table 3 . Green tea displayed the highest TAC value while pomelo juice showed the lowest TAC value. The relative standard deviation (RSD) of the obtained TAC value was 3.2% to 9.9% in different samples, indicating the determination method has a good repeatability. What's more, their TAC values were monitored again after the addition of AA with different and known concentration into the real samples. The results from the detection were named as Equivalent AA Found. the recovery rates of adding AA in the fruit and tea samples were ranging from 88.4% to 114.2%, revealing that the excellent performance of CCP-HA Pdots for determination of TAC in practical application.

In order to examine its potential in the assay of food quality, CCP-HA Pdots were further used to evaluate the stability of total antioxidant activity (representative substance: AA) in real samples under different time and storing conditions. As shown in Fig. 5B, the stability of antioxidants in fruits juices and teas with time (0, 1, 2, 3 and 6 h) under room temperature and daylight were calculated by monitoring fluorescence change of CCP-HA Pdots. The fluorescent intensity gradually decreased with time, which indicated the content of AA in fruit juices and teas also was gradually reduced. Meanwhile, the stability of AA in lemon juice and green tea under different storage conditions were shown in Fig. 5C and D, these results demonstrated that low temperature, avoiding light and a short period of storage are important influence factors in improving stability of antioxidants in fruits juices and teas.

In summary, CCP-HA polymer dots were synthesized by one-step nanoprecipitation method. The morphologies, particle size, surface charge, functional groups and optical features were characterized. It was found that the addition of NaClO and antioxidants can result in the quenched and recovered fluorescence of Pdots, we have proved that this was based on the reversible surface redox process of Pdots. This intriguing phenomenon functions as the determination strategy of TAC of food samples. We successfully applied CCP-HA Pdots to determinate antioxidants in fruit and tea samples with satisfactory results and investigated the stability of antioxidants in real samples including the effect of time and storage conditions. The advantages of this assay method are repeatable determination, quick response, wide detection range and low detection limit. What remains to be improved in this method is the fluorescence wavelength, as the red or near infrared fluorescence of Pdots could further decrease the background signal in the real simples. We expected that CCP-HA Pdots and its reversible surface redox mechanism could pave the way for research of novel biosensors for other targets and contribute to develop advanced assay kit for real samples.

Wei Zhao: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Writing - original draft. Yun Xiang: Formal analysis, Writing - original draft. Jiabin Xu: Visualization, Investigation. Xiaofeng He: Supervision, Funding acquisition. Peng Zhao: Supervision, Funding acquisition, Writing - review & editing.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Interventional Oncology Research Fund of China Health Promotion Foundation (XM_2018_011_0006_01), Pearl River S&T Nova Program of Guangzhou (201806010170) .

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.microc.2020.104805.
